title: The Customs Officer
image_description: 3D clay style, first-person view of a high-tech airport customs booth. A serious officer in a blue uniform is looking down at a passport. Soft cinematic lighting.
strategic_brief: Speed > Politeness. Officers want nouns, not stories. Answer only what is asked.
cloze_setup: In this situation, I should be [______] and avoid [______].
scenario_text: The officer looks up and asks: 'What is the purpose of your visit?'

options.text: I am here for work, but I hope to see some sights too!
options.feedback: The officer is sighing. Too much info. Try to be more noun-focused.

options.text: Business. I'm attending a conference for two weeks.
options.feedback: Stamp! You're through. Excellent directness.

x_ray: Americans in authority roles prioritize efficiency. Short answers signal that you have nothing to hide.

title: The Uber Greeting
image_description: 3D clay style, view from the sidewalk looking into an open back door of a clean white car. A friendly driver is waving from the front seat.
strategic_brief: The 'Vibe Check.' A brief, friendly acknowledgement allows you to sit in silence later.
cloze_setup: A quick [______] is required, but a long [______] is optional.
scenario_text: You get in. The driver says: 'Hey! How's your morning going so far?'

options.text: Good, thanks! How about you?
options.feedback: Perfect balance. You showed politeness without starting a 30-minute life story.

options.text: (Silence) Go to this address, please.
options.feedback: Ouch. This feels cold and robotic. Drivers in the US expect a 'human' moment first.

x_ray: The 'How are you' is a social handshake. Once you complete it, the 'contract' is signed and you can check your phone.

title: Hotel Check-in
image_description: 3D clay style, a high-end hotel lobby with a marble counter. A smiling receptionist with a name tag is standing behind a computer.
strategic_brief: The 'Special Request.' In the US, it is okay to ask for a better experience if you are polite.
cloze_setup: I can [______] for a better room if I am [______].
scenario_text: The receptionist says: 'I have you in room 302.' You wanted a higher floor. What do you say?

options.text: Is there any chance a room on a higher floor is available?
options.feedback: Perfect. 'Is there any chance' is the magic phrase for American requests.

options.text: 302 is too low. Give me a better room.
options.feedback: Too aggressive. You'll get the worst room in the hotel with that tone.

x_ray: Customer service in the US is a negotiation. If you use 'Soft Openers' like 'Is there any chance...', people want to help you.

title: The First Tip
image_description: 3D clay style, a bellhop holding two suitcases next to a hotel room door. He is smiling expectantly but politely.
strategic_brief: The Hand-off. Tipping for physical help is immediate and cash-based.
cloze_setup: Tipping is a [______] for someone's [______].
scenario_text: The bellhop finishes placing your bags. It’s time for the tip. How do you handle it?

options.text: Hand him $5 and say: 'Thanks for the help, I appreciate it!'
options.feedback: Spot on. The verbal 'Thank you' is just as important as the cash.

options.text: Hand him the money silently and look away.
options.feedback: A bit awkward. It makes the tip feel like a bribe rather than a thank you.

x_ray: Tipping is the grease that makes the US gears turn. It acknowledges the person, not just the service.

title: Ordering Coffee
image_description: 3D clay style, a busy cafe counter with a menu board. A young barista with a nose ring and a green apron is waiting.
strategic_brief: The Modification. Customizing your order is not annoying; it’s expected.
cloze_setup: Ordering coffee is about [______] and [______].
scenario_text: The barista asks: 'What can I get for you?' You want a Latte with oat milk.

options.text: Can I get a Latte with oat milk, please?
options.feedback: Simple and clean. Using 'Can I get' is the most natural local way to order.

options.text: I want one coffee with the milk from the oats.
options.feedback: A bit clunky. 'Can I get' flows better than 'I want'.

x_ray: In the US, 'Can I get' is a polite command. It sounds confident but friendly.

title: The Grocery Aisle
image_description: 3D clay style, a grocery store aisle with colorful cereal boxes. Another shopper is blocking the shelf you need to reach.
strategic_brief: The 3-Foot Rule. Respecting personal space is the highest social law in the US.
cloze_setup: I should use the phrase [______] to enter someone's [______].
scenario_text: Someone is standing in front of the cereal you want. How do you get past?

options.text: Wait silently until they move.
options.feedback: Safe, but you might be waiting a long time. It can also feel creepy to stand there.

options.text: Say 'Excuse me' with a small smile and reach in.
options.feedback: Perfect. 'Excuse me' is the universal permission slip in America.

x_ray: 'Excuse me' doesn't mean you did something wrong; it means 'I am about to enter your personal bubble.'

title: The Checkout Line
image_description: 3D clay style, a grocery checkout lane. A cashier is scanning items. A small screen shows the total price.
strategic_brief: The 'Did you find everything?' Script. This is a ritual, not a survey.
cloze_setup: This question is a [______], not a real [______].
scenario_text: The cashier asks: 'Did you find everything okay today?'

options.text: Yep, I'm all set! Thanks.
options.feedback: Exactly right. Fast and positive keeps the line moving.

options.text: Actually, I couldn't find the specific organic eggs I like...
options.feedback: Too late! The cashier can't help you now. This just slows down the line.

x_ray: Service questions at the *end* of an interaction are just social 'closers.' Just say yes and move on.

title: The 'How are you?' Trap
image_description: 3D clay style, walking past a coworker or neighbor in a hallway. They are walking the opposite direction and don't stop.
strategic_brief: The Drive-by Greeting. 'How are you' is often just another way of saying 'Hello.'
cloze_setup: When someone is [______], 'How are you' is just a [______].
scenario_text: A neighbor walks past you quickly and says 'Hey, how's it going?' as they keep walking.

options.text: Good! How are you?
options.feedback: Perfect. You mirrored their energy. They didn't expect you to stop.

options.text: Stop walking and start explaining your day.
options.feedback: Awkward! You've trapped them in a conversation they didn't want.

x_ray: If the person's feet are moving, the question is a greeting. If their feet are still, it's a conversation.

title: Public Transit Rules
image_description: 3D clay style, inside a subway car or bus. People are sitting with headphones on, looking at their phones.
strategic_brief: The Invisible Wall. In public transit, 'polite' means 'leaving people alone.'
cloze_setup: Public transit is a [______] space where people want [______].
scenario_text: You sit down next to someone. Should you try to make small talk?

options.text: No. Just sit down and look at your phone or the window.
options.feedback: Correct. Americans value their 'commute bubble' very highly.

options.text: Yes, ask them how their day is going to be friendly.
options.feedback: This makes people nervous in big US cities. It feels like you want something from them.

x_ray: In a crowded city, giving someone silence is the greatest gift you can offer.

title: The Solo Dinner
image_description: 3D clay style, a warm restaurant interior with a bar area and tables. A host is waiting to seat you.
strategic_brief: Bar vs. Table. Where you sit determines your 'Social Availability.'
cloze_setup: Sitting at the [______] is for talking; the [______] is for privacy.
scenario_text: The host asks: 'Just one? Would you like a table or a spot at the bar?'

options.text: The bar is fine, thanks.
options.feedback: Great choice if you want to be part of the 'vibe' and maybe chat with the bartender.

options.text: I'd prefer a table, please.
options.feedback: Great choice if you want to work or be in your own world.

x_ray: There is no 'wrong' answer here, but knowing the difference is key. The bar is the 'Social Wing' of American dining.

title: The Math of Respect
image_description: 3D clay style, first-person view of a digital tablet screen at a coffee shop showing tip options: 18%, 20%, 25%, and 'No Tip'. A hand is hovering over the screen.
strategic_brief: The 'iPad Flip' Anxiety. Tipping is a performance of gratitude. Choosing 'No Tip' for over-the-counter service is okay, but 20% is the 'Local' standard for seated service.
cloze_setup: Tipping is a [______] standard, not just a [______].
scenario_text: The barista turns the screen to you. You just ordered a black coffee. What is your move?

options.text: Select 20% and smile.
options.feedback: Generous and very 'Local.' It builds instant rapport with the staff.

options.text: Select 'No Tip' for a simple black coffee.
options.feedback: Acceptable for counter service, but you might feel the 'Social Chill.' Use with caution.

x_ray: Americans tip to be seen as 'Good People.' It's as much about your reputation as it is about the barista's wages.

title: The Refill Culture
image_description: 3D clay style, a restaurant table with a half-empty glass of soda. A waiter is walking by with a pitcher of water/soda.
strategic_brief: Abundance Logic. In the US, basic drinks (Water, Soda, Drip Coffee) are usually bottomless. You don't need to ask every time.
cloze_setup: Refills are often [______], so I don't need to be [______].
scenario_text: Your glass is half-empty. You want more, but you're worried about the cost. How do you handle it?

options.text: Wait for the server to notice. If they offer, say 'Yes, please!'
options.feedback: Perfect. This is the natural flow of American dining.

options.text: Call out: 'How much for one more soda?'
options.feedback: Awkward. It signals you don't understand the 'Abundance' rule. Most refills are free!

x_ray: The 'Free Refill' is a symbol of American hospitality. Don't overthink the cost; just enjoy the hydration.

title: The Retail Return
image_description: 3D clay style, a customer service desk at a large store. A clerk is standing behind a register. The user is holding a boxed item.
strategic_brief: The Right to Change Your Mind. In the US, 'The Customer is Always Right' is the law. You don't need a deep excuse to return something.
cloze_setup: I can be [______] about my return without being [______].
scenario_text: The clerk asks: 'Is there anything wrong with the item, or did you just change your mind?'

options.text: It just didn't work for me. I'd like a refund, please.
options.feedback: Perfect. Simple, honest, and fast for the clerk.

options.text: I am so sorry to bother you, I feel bad but I don't need it...
options.feedback: Too much emotion. Returns are a business transaction, not a personal favor.

x_ray: In America, your money is your power. If you aren't happy, you take your power back. No apology needed.

title: The Pharmacy Script
image_description: 3D clay style, a pharmacy counter. A pharmacist in a white coat is looking at a computer screen. Signs for 'Pick Up' and 'Drop Off' are visible.
strategic_brief: The Identity Check. Picking up medicine is a rapid-fire exchange of personal data (Name/Birthday).
cloze_setup: Speed is key. I should lead with my [______] and [______].
scenario_text: The pharmacist says: 'Hi, picking up?'

options.text: Yes, for Han. H-A-N. Birthday is January 1st, 1990.
options.feedback: Excellent. Spelling your name and giving the date immediately saves 30 seconds of typing.

options.text: Yes, I have a prescription from my doctor for the cold medicine.
options.feedback: The pharmacist doesn't care about the doctor yet. They need your name to find the file.

x_ray: At the pharmacy, you are a record in a database. Help the human find the record as fast as possible.

title: The Haircut Vibe
image_description: 3D clay style, sitting in a barber chair looking at a mirror. A barber with tattoos is holding a comb and scissors.
strategic_brief: The Visual Goal. Don't describe the process (cut 2 inches); describe the result (clean and professional).
cloze_setup: I should describe the [______] I want, not just the [______].
scenario_text: The barber asks: 'So, what are we doing today?'

options.text: I want something clean and low-maintenance. Shorter on the sides.
options.feedback: Great. Using 'Low-maintenance' or 'Clean' gives the barber a 'Vibe' to work with.

options.text: Please cut exactly 1.5 centimeters everywhere.
options.feedback: Barbers hate this. It’s too robotic and doesn't account for your head shape.

x_ray: American service providers consider themselves 'Artists.' Give them a vision, not a math problem.

title: The Polite Nudge
image_description: 3D clay style, a restaurant table where the food is 20 minutes late. Other tables are eating. The server is walking by.
strategic_brief: The 'Check-in.' If service is slow, you have the right to ask, but keep the 'Vibe' collaborative, not accusatory.
cloze_setup: I should ask for an [______], not a [______].
scenario_text: You've been waiting for a long time. How do you catch the server's attention?

options.text: Excuse me, just wanted to check on our order. No rush!
options.feedback: Perfect. 'Just wanted to check' is a soft nudge that gets results without making enemies.

options.text: Why is our food so late? Everyone else is eating.
options.feedback: Too aggressive. This makes the server want to avoid your table even more.

x_ray: In the US, 'No rush!' usually means 'Please hurry up, but I'm being nice about it.'

title: The Delivery Directions
image_description: 3D clay style, holding a smartphone showing a map app. A text box from a delivery driver says: 'Can't find your building.'
strategic_brief: Landmark Logic. Americans navigate by 'big things' (next to the red sign), not just numbers.
cloze_setup: I should give the driver a [______] to help them [______].
scenario_text: The driver is lost. What do you text them?

options.text: I'm in building 4, right next to the big blue dumpster.
options.feedback: Excellent. Specific visual markers are better than numbers in a dark parking lot.

options.text: Read the address in the app again. It is correct.
options.feedback: Useless and rude. The driver is already trying that.

x_ray: Helping someone do their job is the fastest way to get what you want. Be a 'Co-Pilot'.

title: Deflecting the Sales Pitch
image_description: 3D clay style, a gym lobby. A very fit staff member with a clipboard is trying to sell a premium membership.
strategic_brief: The Firm Boundary. Salespeople in the US are trained to be 'Aggressively Friendly.' You must be 'Friendly but Firm.'
cloze_setup: I can say [______] without feeling [______].
scenario_text: The staff member says: 'But for just $10 more, you get the sauna! Don't you want the sauna?'

options.text: I'm good with the basic plan for now, but thanks for the offer!
options.feedback: Perfect. 'I'm good' is the polite American way to say 'Stop selling to me.'

options.text: Maybe... let me think about it and tell you next month.
options.feedback: Weak. They will call you every day now. A soft 'Maybe' is a 'Yes' to a salesperson.

x_ray: In America, 'No' is a complete sentence. You don't owe anyone an explanation for your bank account.

title: The iPad Flip Anxiety
image_description: 3D clay style, a person standing at a fast-casual salad shop. The worker is looking at them while they decide on the tip screen.
strategic_brief: The Social Pressure. The iPad tip screen is designed to make you feel watched. Stay calm.
cloze_setup: The tip screen is a [______], not a [______].
scenario_text: You are at a Subway-style shop. They just handed you a sandwich. The 20% tip button is staring at you.

options.text: Choose 'Custom Tip' and add $1 or $2.
options.feedback: Fair. For counter service, a small flat tip is often more appropriate than a percentage.

options.text: Panic and click 25% because you feel awkward.
options.feedback: You're overpaying out of fear. This doesn't build 'Social Power'; it just costs you money.

x_ray: If you are standing up while you eat, a 10-15% tip (or a flat $1) is perfectly fine.

title: The Group Bill
image_description: 3D clay style, a dinner table with four people. A single paper check is sitting in the middle. Everyone is reaching for their phones.
strategic_brief: The Venmo Dance. One person usually pays the whole bill, and everyone else 'Venmos' them immediately.
cloze_setup: Splitting the bill is about [______] and [______].
scenario_text: The bill comes. Your new friend says: 'I'll just put it on my card and you guys can Venmo me.'

options.text: Awesome, thanks! I'll send my share right now.
options.feedback: Perfect. Sending the money *before* you leave the table is the highest form of American etiquette.

options.text: Wait, let's calculate exactly who ate the extra appetizer.
options.feedback: Oof. This is seen as 'Cheap' or 'Difficult' in the US. Just split it evenly or close to it.

x_ray: In American groups, 'Social Peace' is worth more than $5. Don't be the person who makes the math difficult.

title: The Garage Door Wave
image_description: 3D clay style, first-person view from a driveway. A neighbor is standing across the street by their mailbox, looking toward you.
strategic_brief: The 3-Second Rule. In the US, a hand wave is a social 'Ping.' It says 'I see you and we are cool,' without requiring a conversation.
cloze_setup: A wave is a [______] acknowledgement that protects my [______].
scenario_text: You are getting mail and see your neighbor across the street. They look at you. What do you do?

options.text: Give a short wave and a small nod, then go inside.
options.feedback: Perfect. You acknowledged them without 'trapping' either of you in a talk.

options.text: Look down at your mail and pretend you didn't see them.
options.feedback: Awkward. In suburbs, 'Ignoring' is seen as 'Hostile.' A wave is easier.

x_ray: The 'Suburb Wave' is a safety signal. It tells the neighbor: 'I am a friendly resident, not a stranger to worry about.'

title: The Mailroom Chat
image_description: 3D clay style, a modern apartment mailroom with rows of silver lockers. A neighbor is struggling with a large Amazon box.
strategic_brief: The Shared Space. Small talk in mailrooms is 100% about the environment (the heat, the packages, the elevator).
cloze_setup: I should talk about the [______] rather than [______] questions.
scenario_text: You're both getting mail. The neighbor says: 'Man, the AC is totally broken in the lobby today.'

options.text: Tell them your name, where you work, and how much your rent is.
options.feedback: Too much! This is a 30-second interaction. Don't go deep.

options.text: I know, right? It's like a sauna out there. Hope they fix it soon!
options.feedback: Spot on. Validating their complaint about the building is the #1 bonding trick.

x_ray: Americans love 'Complaining Up' (complaining about the weather or the building management) to build instant rapport with strangers.

title: The Dog Park Intro
image_description: 3D clay style, a fenced-in dog park with green grass. A golden retriever is sniffing your dog. The owner is standing 5 feet away.
strategic_brief: The Proxy Friend. In a dog park, you don't talk to the human; you talk to the dog *through* the human.
cloze_setup: The dog is the [______] for the [______].
scenario_text: The other owner is looking at your dog. How do you start the talk?

options.text: Ask: 'How old is he? He's so energetic!'
options.feedback: Perfect. Asking about the dog's age or breed is the 'Safe Entry' for dog owners.

options.text: Ask: 'Do you live in that expensive house on the corner?'
options.feedback: Creepy. Keep the focus on the pets, not the property values.

x_ray: In the US, people are very protective of their privacy, but they are 100% open about their pets. Use the pet as your 'Social Passport'.

title: The Utility Call
image_description: 3D clay style, holding a phone to your ear. The screen shows 'Electricity Co. - On Hold'. You need to fix a billing error.
strategic_brief: The Polite Persistence. Customer service agents handle angry people all day. Being the 'Nice One' gets you better results.
cloze_setup: Being [______] makes the agent want to [______] me.
scenario_text: The agent finally answers: 'How can I help you today?'

options.text: Hi! I'm sorry, I think there's a mistake on my bill. Could you help me look at it?
options.feedback: Great. Using 'Could you help me' makes the agent an ally, not an enemy.

options.text: Your company stole $50 from me. Fix it now.
options.feedback: They will put you back on hold for another hour. Never attack the person on the phone.

x_ray: The phrase 'Could you help me understand...' is a power move. It forces them to look at the data without feeling blamed.

title: The Trash Rules
image_description: 3D clay style, a sidewalk with a Blue (Recycle) bin and a Black (Trash) bin. A neighbor is watching you from their window.
strategic_brief: The 'Good Citizen' Test. In many US neighborhoods, how you handle your trash is how neighbors judge your character.
cloze_setup: Following the [______] shows I respect the [______].
scenario_text: You have a large cardboard box. What is the 'Pro' move?

options.text: Break it down flat and put it inside the Blue bin.
options.feedback: Perfect. A flat box is the sign of a 'High-Vibe' neighbor.

options.text: Just throw the whole big box on top of the bin.
options.feedback: This is the #1 way to get 'The Side-eye' from your neighbors. It looks messy.

x_ray: Americans view their neighborhood as a shared product. If you keep the product 'clean,' you are welcomed into the club.

title: The Nextdoor Drama
image_description: 3D clay style, a smartphone screen showing a neighborhood app. A post says: 'Suspicious car parked on the street. Anyone know them?'
strategic_brief: The Digital Fence. Neighborhood apps are for 'Observation,' not for joining every argument.
cloze_setup: I should [______] more than I [______] on these apps.
scenario_text: You see people arguing about a local park in the app. What do you do?

options.text: Read the comments to learn the local culture, but stay silent.
options.feedback: Smart move. You get the 'Intel' without making enemies before you've even met them.

options.text: Post your strong opinion and tell everyone they are wrong.
options.feedback: Dangerous. You'll be 'That Person' in the neighborhood before you even walk your dog.

x_ray: Neighborhood apps are 'Digital Anthropology.' Use them to see what people care about (Crime, Dogs, Property), but don't get 'muddy'.

title: Becoming a 'Regular'
image_description: 3D clay style, a cozy local cafe. The barista recognizes you as you walk in.
strategic_brief: The Third Place. A 'Regular' is someone the staff knows. It’s the first step to feeling like you belong in a city.
cloze_setup: Being a 'Regular' provides a sense of [______] in a new [______].
scenario_text: The barista says: 'Hey! The usual today?'

options.text: Yes, please! How's your morning going?
options.feedback: You've done it. You've unlocked 'Regular' status. This makes a new city feel like home.

options.text: Actually, I want something different today. (Then explain it coldly).
options.feedback: Fine, but you missed the chance to build that 'Small Town' connection.

x_ray: In a lonely world, being recognized at your local shop is 'Social Gold.' Keep that relationship warm.

title: The Noise Complaint
image_description: 3D clay style, an apartment hallway at 11 PM. Loud music is coming from the neighbor's door. You have work tomorrow.
strategic_brief: The 'Soft' Conflict. Americans hate direct confrontation with neighbors. Use the 'Help' frame.
cloze_setup: I should frame the noise as an [______] rather than an [______].
scenario_text: You knock on the neighbor's door. They open it. What is your line?

options.text: Hey, sorry to bug you! The walls are just super thin—could you turn it down a bit?
options.feedback: Perfect. Blaming the 'Thin Walls' (The Building) instead of the 'Loud Music' (The Person) avoids a fight.

options.text: Be quiet! I am trying to sleep and you are being rude.
options.feedback: High risk. This creates a 'War' in the hallway that lasts for months.

x_ray: The 'Blame the Third Party' trick (The Walls, The Rules, The Manager) allows you to get what you want without the neighbor hating you.

title: The 'Stop By' Lie
image_description: 3D clay style, a neighbor waving while walking away. They say: 'We should grab a drink sometime! Stop by whenever!'
strategic_brief: The Politeness Trap. 'Stop by whenever' almost always means 'Don't stop by without a text first.'
cloze_setup: 'Whenever' is a [______] phrase, not a [______].
scenario_text: They just said 'Stop by whenever!' How do you actually handle it?

options.text: Wait a week, then text: 'Hey! Still down for that drink? When works for you?'
options.feedback: Correct. You called their 'bluff' politely by moving it to text.

options.text: Actually knock on their door 10 minutes later.
options.feedback: Social Disaster. You just gave them a 'Home Invasion' heart attack. Never take 'Whenever' literally.

x_ray: In the US, 'Whenever' is a way to end a conversation warmly. It is a 'Vibe,' not a 'Time'.

title: The Housewarming Invite
image_description: 3D clay style, a neighbor handing you a physical card or showing you an invite on their phone for a BBQ.
strategic_brief: The 'Plus One' & The Gift. Never show up empty-handed to a residential invite.
cloze_setup: I should always [______] what I can [______] to the party.
scenario_text: They invite you over for a BBQ. What is your follow-up question?

options.text: That sounds awesome! What can I bring? Drinks? A side dish?
options.feedback: Perfect. This signals you are a 'High-Value' guest who understands American hosting.

options.text: Okay, what time is the food ready?
options.feedback: You sound like a customer, not a guest. This is a party, not a restaurant.

x_ray: Even if they say 'Nothing!', bring a 6-pack of soda/beer or a bag of chips. It’s the 'Entry Fee' for American friendship.

title: The 15-Second Bio
image_description: 3D clay style, first-person view standing in a bright modern office with a standing desk. A smiling coworker in a branded hoodie is holding a coffee and looking at you.
strategic_brief: The 'Value-First' Intro. In the US, your name is less important than what you 'Do' for the team. Keep it short and high-energy.
cloze_setup: I should lead with my [______] and one [______] detail.
scenario_text: A new teammate walks up: 'Hey! I'm Sarah from Design. You're the new dev, right?'

options.text: Hi Sarah! I'm Han. I'm joining the backend team. Just moved here from Seoul!
options.feedback: Perfect. You gave your Name, Role, and a 'Conversation Hook' (Seoul).

options.text: My name is Dong Lak Han. I have 10 years of experience in Java and Python.
options.feedback: A bit too formal. This sounds like a resume, not a greeting. Save the '10 years' for the LinkedIn profile.

x_ray: American 'Work-Talk' is a mix of 80% professional and 20% personal. Giving a small personal fact (where you are from) makes you 'Safe' to talk to.

title: The Watercooler Pivot
image_description: 3D clay style, an office breakroom with a high-end coffee machine. Two coworkers are laughing about a TV show. You are standing nearby.
strategic_brief: The Open Circle. If people are standing in a way that leaves a gap, you are invited to listen, but not necessarily to speak yet.
cloze_setup: I can [______] the group without [______] the flow.
scenario_text: Two coworkers are talking about a crazy football game. You know nothing about football. What do you do?

options.text: Stand near them, nod, and smile. If they look at you, say 'Sounds like a wild game!'
options.feedback: Great. You 'Validated' the vibe without needing to be an expert. You're now part of the circle.

options.text: Walk up and ask: 'What are the rules of football? I want to learn.'
options.feedback: Too heavy. You just killed their fun conversation by turning it into a lesson. Just 'Vibe' for now.

x_ray: Joining a group isn't about what you say; it's about matching their 'Energy Level.' If they are excited, look excited.

title: The 'Slack' Vibe
image_description: 3D clay style, a close-up of a laptop screen showing a Slack channel. A message from a teammate says: 'Hey! You got a sec to look at this bug?'
strategic_brief: The Digital Handshake. Slack is 'Business Casual.' Use emojis to show you aren't an angry robot.
cloze_setup: I should use [______] to soften the [______] of my text.
scenario_text: You are busy, but you can help in 10 minutes. How do you reply?

options.text: I am busy right now. 10 minutes.
options.feedback: Too blunt! In text, this sounds like you are shouting. It feels cold.

options.text: Hey! Just finishing something up—I'll ping you in 10! 👍
options.feedback: Perfect. The '👍' and 'Hey!' make you sound helpful and approachable.

x_ray: In US digital culture, 'No Emoji = Angry.' Adding a simple thumbs-up or a smile is like a 'Social Insurance' policy.

title: The Pre-Meeting 2 Minutes
image_description: 3D clay style, a Zoom/Teams call screen with 4 avatars. People are waiting for the Host to start. One person is talking about their weekend.
strategic_brief: The 'Dead Air' Rule. Americans hate silence. The 2 minutes before a meeting starts is for 'Relationship Maintenance.'
cloze_setup: Small talk before a [______] builds [______] with the team.
scenario_text: Everyone is waiting. Someone asks you: 'Han, how's the new apartment coming along?'

options.text: It's good. (Silence)
options.feedback: Conversation Killer. You just made the whole Zoom call feel 'Awkward.'

options.text: Good! Finally got a couch yesterday. It's starting to feel like home!
options.feedback: Perfect. You gave a small detail that others can comment on. You kept the 'Vibe' alive.

x_ray: Being 'Good at your job' in America includes being 'Good to be around.' Don't skip the small talk.

title: The 'Reply All' Etiquette
image_description: 3D clay style, an email inbox on a screen. 50 people are on a thread saying 'Congrats!' to a coworker. Your mouse is over the reply button.
strategic_brief: The Noise Filter. Don't be the person who fills 50 inboxes with a 'Me too' message.
cloze_setup: I should [______] the sender [______] if my message is just a 'Congrats'.
scenario_text: Your boss sends an email to the whole department announcing a promotion. Everyone is hitting 'Reply All' to say 'Congrats!'. What do you do?

options.text: Hit 'Reply All' and say 'Congrats! So well deserved!'
options.feedback: Acceptable, but annoying to many people. You're adding to the 'Digital Noise'.

options.text: Send a separate, private email to the person: 'Hey! Saw the news, huge congrats!'
options.feedback: High Vibe. It feels more personal to them and keeps the rest of the team's inbox clean.

x_ray: Respecting people's 'Inbox Space' is a hidden way to show you are a high-level professional.

title: The Coffee Run
image_description: 3D clay style, standing at your desk, putting on a jacket. Your coworker is looking at you.
strategic_brief: The 'Favor' Loop. Offering to grab a coffee for a teammate is the fastest way to turn a 'Coworker' into a 'Work Friend.'
cloze_setup: Offering a [______] is a low-cost way to build [______].
scenario_text: You are going to the breakroom or Starbucks. How do you phrase the offer?

options.text: Hey, I'm grabbing a coffee. Want me to get you one?
options.feedback: Perfect. Simple and low-pressure. Even if they say no, they will appreciate that you asked.

options.text: I am going to get coffee. Do you need anything?
options.feedback: Good, but 'Want me to get you one?' sounds a bit more like a friendly offer.

x_ray: In the US, 'Favors' aren't about the coffee; they are about showing that you are 'Thinking of the Team.'

title: The Desk Stop
image_description: 3D clay style, walking up to a coworker's desk. They have headphones on and are typing quickly.
strategic_brief: The Visual Cue. Headphones are the universal 'Do Not Disturb' sign. If you must interrupt, use a 'Soft Opener.'
cloze_setup: I should [______] their [______] before I start talking.
scenario_text: You need a quick answer on a project. They are wearing headphones. What do you do?

options.text: Tap them on the shoulder and start talking immediately.
options.feedback: Social Disaster. Never touch a coworker to get their attention. It's seen as aggressive.

options.text: Stand 3 feet away, wait for them to look up, and say: 'Hey, sorry to bug you! Got a quick sec?'
options.feedback: Perfect. You respected their bubble and gave them the option to say 'Later'.

x_ray: The phrase 'Got a quick sec?' is the 'Escape Hatch' for Americans. It lets them finish their thought before they talk to you.

title: Decoding 'Business Casual'
image_description: 3D clay style, standing in front of a mirror at home. You have a suit, a hoodie, and a polo shirt laid out on the bed.
strategic_brief: The Middle Ground. In modern US offices, being 'Over-dressed' is sometimes as bad as being 'Under-dressed.'
cloze_setup: It's better to be [______] slightly more [______] than everyone else.
scenario_text: The company says the vibe is 'Casual.' What do you wear for your first big presentation?

options.text: A full suit and tie.
options.feedback: Too much. You'll make everyone else feel uncomfortable. You look like you're trying too hard.

options.text: Dark jeans and a nice button-down shirt or polo.
options.feedback: The Sweet Spot. It shows you care about the meeting but you 'get' the company culture.

x_ray: American style is about 'Effortless Competence.' You want to look like you put in effort, but not like you're worried about it.

title: The Birthday Celebration
image_description: 3D clay style, a group of coworkers gathered around a desk with a store-bought cake. Someone is lighting candles.
strategic_brief: The 'Mandatory Fun.' Even if you find it cheesy, participating in office celebrations is how you prove you are a 'Cultural Fit.'
cloze_setup: Participating in [______] rituals builds [______] with the group.
scenario_text: Everyone is singing 'Happy Birthday' to a coworker you don't know very well. What do you do?

options.text: Stay at your desk and keep working to show you are productive.
options.feedback: Wrong. You look like a 'Robot.' People will think you are 'Too good' for the team.

options.text: Stand with the group, clap, and say 'Happy Birthday!' at the end.
options.feedback: Perfect. You don't have to be the loudest person, but you must be 'Present'.

x_ray: In the US, 'The Team' is a family-lite. If the family is celebrating, you show up for 5 minutes.

title: The Team Lunch
image_description: 3D clay style, a large round table at a casual Mexican restaurant. 6 coworkers are sitting around it, laughing and ordering chips.
strategic_brief: The Safe Zone. A team lunch is a test of your 'Humanity.' Talk about hobbies, food, and movies—not the project due at 5 PM.
cloze_setup: Team lunches are for [______] building, not [______] updates.
scenario_text: The conversation dies down for a second. What do you say?

options.text: So, how is the Q4 budget report looking, Sarah?
options.feedback: Ugh! You just brought 'Work' to the 'Lunch.' Everyone is now sighing internally.

options.text: Has anyone tried that new pizza place down the street? I heard it's great.
options.feedback: Excellent. Food is the ultimate 'Safe Topic.' It keeps the mood light and fun.

x_ray: At lunch, you aren't an Engineer; you are a Person who eats. Focus on the 'Person' part.

title: The 'That’s Interesting' Trap
image_description: 3D clay style, a glass-walled conference room. A senior manager with glasses is leaning back, looking at your presentation slide with a neutral expression.
strategic_brief: The Polite Pivot. In the US, 'Interesting' is a neutral word used to avoid saying 'I don't like this' or 'This is weird.'
cloze_setup: I should [______] their [______] before I assume they agree.
scenario_text: You just finished sharing a bold new idea. Your boss says: 'Hmm. That's a very... interesting perspective, Han.'

options.text: Thanks! I'm glad you find it interesting. Should I start the next phase?
options.feedback: Stop! You missed the 'Vibe.' They aren't convinced. If you move forward now, you'll look out of touch.

options.text: Thanks. Is there a specific part you'd like me to dive deeper into, or any concerns?
options.feedback: Perfect. You recognized the 'Soft No' and opened the door for honest feedback.

x_ray: When an American says 'Interesting' without a smile, it usually means 'I disagree, but I don't want to hurt your feelings yet.'

title: Professional Aggression
image_description: 3D clay style, a smartphone screen showing an email notification. The subject line is 'RE: Project Update' and the first line is visible.
strategic_brief: The Email Sword. Phrases like 'Per my last email' are the American way of saying 'You aren't listening and I'm annoyed.'
cloze_setup: I should [______] the [______] rather than getting defensive.
scenario_text: A teammate emails you: 'Per my last email, the deadline was actually yesterday. Do you have the files?'

options.text: Sorry for the mix-up! I missed that note. Sending them over now.
options.feedback: Good. You de-escalated the 'Vibe' by owning the mistake quickly.

options.text: You don't need to be so rude. I am working as fast as I can.
options.feedback: Disaster. You just turned a 'Work Error' into a 'Personal War.' Stay professional.

x_ray: 'Per my last email' is a 'Yellow Card' in the office. It means: 'I've already told you this, don't make me tell you again.'

title: The 'Soft' Deadline
image_description: 3D clay style, a coworker standing by your desk, holding a laptop. They are looking at you with a casual, relaxed posture.
strategic_brief: The Urgency Mask. Americans hate to sound like 'Dictators.' They use soft phrases for hard requirements.
cloze_setup: I should [______] a [______] even if they didn't give me one.
scenario_text: A coworker drops a task on your desk: 'Hey, could you take a look at this when you have a chance?'

options.text: Sure! Does this need to be done by EOD (End of Day), or can it wait until tomorrow?
options.feedback: Perfect. You translated 'When you have a chance' into a real deadline.

options.text: Okay, I will do it next week when I am less busy.
options.feedback: Risky. To them, 'When you have a chance' often means 'Today or Tomorrow.' Next week is too late.

x_ray: In the US, 'When you have a chance' is a test of your 'Proactiveness.' It's an invitation to show you prioritize their work.

title: The Feedback Sandwich
image_description: 3D clay style, a one-on-one meeting with a manager. They are smiling and nodding, but they have a list of 'Notes' on their iPad.
strategic_brief: The 'But' Pivot. Americans wrap criticism in two layers of praise. You must listen for the 'Middle' layer.
cloze_setup: The [______] is usually found right after the word [______].
scenario_text: Your manager says: 'You've been doing great work on the design, BUT I'd like to see you participate more in meetings. Otherwise, keep it up!'

options.text: Focus on: 'They said I'm doing great work! I don't need to change anything.'
options.feedback: Wrong. You missed the meat of the sandwich. The 'participation' part is what they actually care about.

options.text: Focus on: 'I need to speak up more in meetings. How can I do that better?'
options.feedback: Yes! You ignored the 'Bread' and found the 'Meat.' You are growing.

x_ray: The 'Bread' (Praise) is to keep you happy. The 'Meat' (Criticism) is what gets you promoted.

title: The 'Swing By' Logic
image_description: 3D clay style, a manager walking past your office door. They pause for a split second and point toward their office.
strategic_brief: The Unscheduled Sync. 'Swing by my office later' is a polite way of saying 'We need to talk privately.'
cloze_setup: A 'Swing By' is usually [______], but I should [______] it seriously.
scenario_text: Your boss says: 'Hey Han, swing by my office before you head out today.'

options.text: Go there immediately with your notebook and a positive attitude.
options.feedback: Perfect. Treating 'Informal' requests with 'Formal' preparation shows you are a Power Player.

options.text: Wait until you're walking to your car and just poke your head in: 'You wanted me?'
options.feedback: Too casual. If they asked to see you 'Before you head out,' they likely have something specific to discuss.

x_ray: In the US, 'Swing by' is a way to lower the pressure of a meeting, but the content of the meeting is still important.

title: The 'I Hear You' Signal
image_description: 3D clay style, two coworkers in a debate. One has their arms crossed but is nodding slowly while the other person talks.
strategic_brief: Acknowledgment vs. Agreement. Americans will often 'Validate' your feelings as a way to say 'I'm done listening to your argument.'
cloze_setup: 'I hear you' means they [______] my words, not necessarily my [______].
scenario_text: You are arguing for a change. Your coworker says: 'I hear what you're saying, Han. Let's move on to the next topic.'

options.text: Keep arguing because they said they 'Hear' you, so you think you're winning.
options.feedback: Stop! They just 'Hard-Closed' the conversation. Pushing further will make you look 'Difficult.'

options.text: Stop, nod, and move on. Re-approach later with more data.
options.feedback: Perfect. You recognized the 'Social Red Light.' Save your energy for a better time.

x_ray: In the US, 'I hear you' is the 'Esc' key on the keyboard. It ends the current process gracefully.

title: The 'Loop In' Meaning
image_description: 3D clay style, a group of coworkers in a huddle. One person is pointing to another person who isn't there.
strategic_brief: The Inclusion Test. 'Looping someone in' is about visibility and political safety, not just sharing info.
cloze_setup: [______] people in is about [______] and transparency.
scenario_text: Your boss says: 'Make sure you loop Sarah in on that email thread.'

options.text: Copy Sarah on the email and add a quick note: 'Sarah, looping you in for visibility.'
options.feedback: Perfect. You followed the 'Political Map' of the office. Sarah feels respected.

options.text: Just forward the final result to Sarah at the end of the week.
options.feedback: Wrong. Your boss wanted Sarah to see the *process*, not just the result. You've now made Sarah feel 'Left out.'

x_ray: In America, 'Visibility' is currency. Looping someone in is like giving them a seat at the table.

title: The Weekend 'Safe' Talk
image_description: 3D clay style, Monday morning in the office. People are standing by their desks with coffee, chatting casually.
strategic_brief: The 'Normalcy' Check. Americans use 'How was your weekend?' to see if you are a relatable human, not to hear your secrets.
cloze_setup: I should share a [______] detail that shows I have a [______] outside of work.
scenario_text: A coworker asks: 'Hey Han! Do anything fun this weekend?'

options.text: Not much, just caught up on some sleep and went to the park. How about you?
options.feedback: Perfect. It's 'Relatable' and 'Safe.' It keeps the 'Vibe' friendly and professional.

options.text: I had a very deep philosophical realization about my purpose in life.
options.feedback: Too heavy! Monday morning is for 'Small Talk,' not 'Soul Talk.' You've made them uncomfortable.

x_ray: The 'Weekend Update' is a social glue. It proves you aren't just a work-robot.

title: The False Invitation
image_description: 3D clay style, two coworkers walking toward the exit. One turns back and waves casually.
strategic_brief: The Future 'Maybe.' Phrases like 'We should grab lunch sometime' are often social decorations, not actual plans.
cloze_setup: 'Sometime' usually means '[______]' unless I [______] it.
scenario_text: A coworker says: 'Hey, we should grab lunch sometime and talk about that project!'

options.text: Awesome! I'll send you a calendar invite for Thursday.
options.feedback: Good. You are 'Calling their bluff' in a professional way. If they say no, you'll know they weren't serious.

options.text: Wait at your desk every day for them to come ask you for lunch.
options.feedback: You'll be waiting forever. They've likely already forgotten they said it.

x_ray: In the US, 'Sometime' is a way to end a conversation on a 'High Note.' It is a 'Vibe,' not a 'Commitment.'

title: The Annual Review Hyperbole
image_description: 3D clay style, an iPad screen showing a self-evaluation form with sliders and text boxes. A hand is holding a stylus.
strategic_brief: The 'Bragging' Requirement. In the US, being 'Humble' during a review is actually a career mistake.
cloze_setup: I should [______] my achievements because my boss [______] expects me to.
scenario_text: The form asks: 'Rate your performance this year from 1 to 5.' You feel like you did a solid, good job.

options.text: Choose '4' and list 3 specific wins. Say: 'I've really grown in [X] and [Y].'
options.feedback: Perfect. In America, if you don't 'Market' yourself, no one else will. A '4' is the 'Honest Pro' score.

options.text: Choose '3' because you don't want to sound arrogant.
options.feedback: Wrong. A '3' in an American review often means 'Average' or 'Replaceable.' Don't undersell yourself.

x_ray: The Annual Review is a 'Sales Pitch' where YOU are the product. If you don't believe in the product, why should they?

title: The Deadpan Detective
image_description: 3D clay style, a coworker standing by a printer that is obviously jammed and smoking. They look at you with a completely straight face and no smile.
strategic_brief: Recognizing Irony. Americans often say the exact opposite of the truth to highlight how obvious a situation is. This is 'Deadpan' humor.
cloze_setup: If the words don't match the [______], it is likely a [______].
scenario_text: The printer is broken. Your coworker looks at it and says: 'Wow, I love how reliable our technology is.'

options.text: Actually, it is not reliable. It is broken right now.
options.feedback: Whoops! You 'Fact-Checked' a joke. This makes you look like you have no sense of humor.

options.text: Laugh and say: 'Yeah, it's a masterpiece of engineering, isn't it?'
options.feedback: Perfect! You played along with the irony. You've officially 'viped' with the teammate.

x_ray: When someone says something obviously false with a straight face, they are inviting you to complain with them through humor.

title: Self-Deprecation Logic
image_description: 3D clay style, a senior executive standing on a small stage at an office meeting. He is pointing at a slide with a small typo and chuckling.
strategic_brief: Lowering the Shield. Americans in power often make fun of their own mistakes to seem 'Relatable' and 'Human.'
cloze_setup: Making fun of [______] is a sign of [______] in the US.
scenario_text: The CEO says: 'As you can see by this typo, I clearly didn't graduate top of my class!' Everyone laughs. What do you do?

options.text: Smile and laugh along with the group.
options.feedback: Correct. You recognized the 'Social Signal'—he is telling everyone it's okay to be human.

options.text: Say loudly: 'Yes, it is very important to check your spelling next time.'
options.feedback: Social Suicide. You just 'attacked' someone who was being vulnerable. Never turn their self-joke into a real criticism.

x_ray: In the US, only confident people make fun of themselves. By laughing, you acknowledge their status and their humility.

title: The Pop Culture Bridge
image_description: 3D clay style, a group of coworkers at a lunch table. One is imitating a famous movie character or mentioning a viral meme.
strategic_brief: The Shared Screen. Americans use TV shows (The Office, Succession) and memes as a common language.
cloze_setup: I don't need to [______] the show to [______] the feeling.
scenario_text: A coworker says: 'This project is starting to feel like a scene from Inception.' You haven't seen the movie. How do you respond?

options.text: I haven't seen that movie. What is it about?
options.feedback: Honest, but it stops the fun. It turns the 'Vibe' into a 'Lesson'.

options.text: Laugh and say: 'Haha, it is getting pretty confusing, right?'
options.feedback: Smart. You used context clues (Inception = confusing/dreamlike) to keep the conversation moving.

x_ray: You don't need to be an expert on Hollywood. Just react to the *emotion* behind the reference.

title: The 'Dad Joke' Test
image_description: 3D clay style, an older manager with a punny t-shirt. He just told a really bad, cheesy joke at the start of a meeting.
strategic_brief: The Groan Ritual. 'Dad jokes' are intentionally bad. The goal is to create a shared 'groan' or lighthearted moment.
cloze_setup: A 'Dad Joke' is meant to be [______], not [______].
scenario_text: Manager: 'Why did the scarecrow win an award? Because he was outstanding in his field!' (Silence).

options.text: Roll your eyes playfully or give a small 'Oh man...' laugh.
options.feedback: Perfect. This is the 'Correct' response to a pun. You are participating in the ritual.

options.text: Stare blankly because you don't think it's funny.
options.feedback: Too serious. You're bringing a 'Cold Vibe' to a moment meant for 'Warmth'.

x_ray: In the US, bad jokes are used to break the tension of a 'Serious' workplace.

title: American Hyperbole
image_description: 3D clay style, a coworker holding a sandwich. They look like they just won the lottery.
strategic_brief: The 'Best Ever' Logic. Americans use extreme words ('Amazing', 'Incredible', 'Life-changing') for very small things.
cloze_setup: I should [______] the [______] of their words by 50%.
scenario_text: Your coworker bites into a taco and says: 'Oh my god, this is literally the greatest thing I have ever eaten in my life.'

options.text: Say: 'Wow, really? Is it better than your wedding dinner?'
options.feedback: Too literal! You're ruining their 'Hype'.

options.text: Smile and say: 'That good, huh? I'll have to try that place!'
options.feedback: Perfect. You matched their 'Hype Vibe' without being literal.

x_ray: Hyperbole is just a way to show 'Enthusiasm.' It’s not a statement of fact.

title: Workplace Slang 101
image_description: 3D clay style, a laptop screen with a Slack message from a younger coworker: 'That presentation was fire. No cap.'
strategic_brief: The Age Gap. Slang is used to show 'Authenticity' and 'Coolness.' Use it sparingly unless you are 100% sure of the meaning.
cloze_setup: I should [______] slang before I try to [______] it.
scenario_text: A junior dev says your code change was 'Goated.' How do you react?

options.text: Say: 'Thanks! I appreciate the support.'
options.feedback: Safe and professional. You acknowledged the praise without trying too hard to sound 'Gen Z'.

options.text: Say: 'Yes, it is very fire, no cap, my bro.'
options.feedback: Cringe! You sound like you are trying way too hard. Stick to your own voice.

x_ray: You don't need to speak like a teenager to be liked. Just 'Understanding' the slang is 90% of the battle.

title: The 'Light' Roast
image_description: 3D clay style, standing with a group of work friends. One friend points at your very bright, colorful new sneakers and grins.
strategic_brief: The Trust Test. In the US, if a friend makes a small, harmless joke about you, it means they finally feel 'Safe' with you.
cloze_setup: A 'Roast' from a [______] is a sign of [______].
scenario_text: Friend: 'Whoa, Han! Are those shoes bright enough? I think I need sunglasses just to look at your feet!' (Everyone laughs).

options.text: Get offended and explain: 'They were very expensive and are high quality.'
options.feedback: Wrong. You just made everyone feel awkward. You 'Failed' the trust test.

options.text: Laugh and say: 'I know, right? I wanted to make sure you guys could find me if I got lost!'
options.feedback: BOOM. You leaned into the joke. You are now 'One of us.'

x_ray: If someone jokes about you, joke with them. It shows you have high 'Social EQ' and aren't easily hurt.

title: Sports as Currency
image_description: 3D clay style, Monday morning by the elevator. Two guys are talking about 'The Game' last night with a lot of energy.
strategic_brief: The Ultimate Safe Topic. You don't need to love sports; you just need to know 'The Narrative' (who won/who lost).
cloze_setup: Sports are a [______] way to build [______] without being personal.
scenario_text: Coworker: 'Can you believe that comeback last night? Unreal!'

options.text: I don't watch sports. I think it is a waste of time.
options.feedback: Social Wall. You just killed the mood and made yourself sound elitist.

options.text: I didn't catch the whole thing, but I saw the highlights! Crazy ending, right?
options.feedback: Perfect. You participated in the 'Energy' without needing to be an expert.

x_ray: In the US, 'The Game' is the default small talk. Knowing the 'Vibe' of the local team is a huge social advantage.

title: Irony vs. Sincerity
image_description: 3D clay style, a coworker gives you a high-five after you finish a long presentation. They say: 'Well, that was a total disaster.' (They are smiling).
strategic_brief: Decoding the Smile. If someone says something negative but is smiling/high-fiving, they mean the opposite.
cloze_setup: The [______] always tells the [______] of the words.
scenario_text: You did a great job. Coworker smiles and says: 'Wow, you really messed that up, Han!'

options.text: Smile back and say: 'I know, I'll try to be even worse next time!'
options.feedback: Perfect irony! You matched their 'Reverse Praise'.

options.text: Look worried and ask: 'Wait, what did I do wrong?'
options.feedback: You missed the irony. Now they feel bad for making a joke and things are awkward.

x_ray: Americans often use 'Negative Irony' to praise someone who is doing so well that it's obvious.

title: The Happy Hour Ritual
image_description: 3D clay style, a dimly lit, cozy bar/restaurant at 5:30 PM. Coworkers are holding drinks (beers/sodas) and standing in a loose circle.
strategic_brief: The Transition Zone. Happy Hour is where 'Work Han' becomes 'Person Han.'
cloze_setup: At Happy Hour, I should [______] about the project and [______] about life.
scenario_text: A coworker brings up the big deadline on Monday while everyone is drinking. What do you do?

options.text: Immediately start talking about the spreadsheet and the budget.
options.feedback: Vibe Killer! Nobody wants to think about Monday at 5:30 PM on Friday.

options.text: Say: 'Let's worry about that on Monday! Right now, I just want to know where you got those shoes!'
options.feedback: Legendary. You 'Protected the Vibe.' Everyone will love you for this.

x_ray: Happy Hour is a 'Sanctuary.' The person who brings 'Stress' into the 'Sanctuary' is never invited back.

title: The Strategic Interrupt
image_description: 3D clay style, a fast-paced Zoom meeting with 6 people. One person is talking with a lot of hand gestures. There is a small gap in their breathing.
strategic_brief: Finding the Gap. In US meetings, waiting for 'Silence' means you'll never speak. You must lean in and claim your space.
cloze_setup: Interrupting is [______] if I am [______] to the conversation.
scenario_text: The Lead is talking quickly about the roadmap. You have a critical technical concern. How do you jump in?

options.text: Wait until the meeting is completely over, then send a long email.
options.feedback: Too late. The decision is already made. You look like you're hiding behind your keyboard.

options.text: Lean forward, raise a hand slightly, and say: 'Can I just jump in with a quick point on that?'
options.feedback: Power Move. 'Can I just jump in' is the polite American signal that you have something important to say.

x_ray: In America, the 'Floor' belongs to whoever takes it. If you wait to be asked, people assume you have nothing to say.

title: The 'I See it Differently' Frame
image_description: 3D clay style, a glass-walled office. You are sitting across from a coworker who just proposed a plan you think will fail. They look confident.
strategic_brief: De-personalizing Conflict. Don't say 'You are wrong.' Say 'The data shows something else.'
cloze_setup: I should attack the [______], not the [______].
scenario_text: Coworker: 'I think we should launch this on Monday no matter what.' What is your response?

options.text: I see it a bit differently. Looking at the test results, I'm worried about the stability. What do you think?
options.feedback: Excellent. 'I see it a bit differently' is the ultimate 'Safe' way to disagree with a peer.

options.text: That is a bad idea. We are not ready and you know it.
options.feedback: Aggressive. You just turned a technical debate into a personal fight. They will stop listening now.

x_ray: By framing disagreement as a 'different perspective' based on 'data,' you allow the other person to change their mind without losing face.

title: Saying 'No' to the Boss
image_description: 3D clay style, your manager is standing at your desk with a new stack of folders. They look stressed and are asking for 'one more thing.'
strategic_brief: The Capacity Argument. A Power Player doesn't say 'No.' They say 'If I do this, what should I stop doing?'
cloze_setup: I should frame my 'No' as a [______] of [______].
scenario_text: Boss: 'Han, I know you're busy, but can you finish this report by 5 PM?'

options.text: I'd love to help with that. If I prioritize this, should I push the project build to tomorrow?
options.feedback: Perfect. You aren't saying 'No'; you are asking the boss to help you manage your time.

options.text: No, I am already too busy. It is impossible.
options.feedback: Too blunt. It sounds like you aren't a 'Team Player.' Always offer a trade-off.

x_ray: American bosses value 'Can-do' attitudes. When you ask to 'Prioritize,' you are still saying 'Yes' to their authority while saying 'No' to the workload.

title: Defending Your Design
image_description: 3D clay style, you are presenting a UI design on a large screen. A stakeholder says: 'I don't like the color blue. Make it red.'
strategic_brief: The Expert Pivot. Don't defend your 'Taste.' Defend the 'User Goal.'
cloze_setup: I should link my [______] to the [______] objective.
scenario_text: Stakeholder: 'Red is my favorite color. Let's change the whole dashboard to red.'

options.text: Okay, you're the boss. I will change it.
options.feedback: Weak. You lost your authority as a designer. They hired you for your expertise, not your 'Yes'.

options.text: Interesting. We chose blue because it tests higher for user trust. Would red still align with that goal?
options.feedback: Brilliant. You used the 'Interesting' pivot and challenged them to prove their idea with data.

x_ray: When you defend an idea with 'Logic' rather than 'Feeling,' you become a strategic partner instead of just a 'worker.'

title: Handling the 'Talk-over'
image_description: 3D clay style, a mid-meeting scene. You are speaking, but a louder coworker starts talking right over you as if you aren't there.
strategic_brief: Reclaiming the Floor. If someone interrupts you, don't stop. Finish your sentence or use a 'Hold' phrase.
cloze_setup: I must [______] my [______] without getting angry.
scenario_text: Coworker: 'Actually, let me stop you there—' (They keep talking). What do you do?

options.text: Stop immediately and look at your notes with a sad face.
options.feedback: You just taught them that they can ignore you. This hurts your career long-term.

options.text: Keep talking for 3 seconds, then say: 'One sec, let me just finish this thought and then I'll hand it over.'
options.feedback: Masterful. You were polite but firm. You held the 'Power' in the room.

x_ray: In the US, 'Niceness' is often confused with 'Weakness.' Holding your ground shows you believe your ideas have value.

title: The Professional Apology
image_description: 3D clay style, you are in a small meeting room. You made a mistake that cost the team a day of work. Everyone is looking at you.
strategic_brief: Accountability over Excuse. A Power Player takes 100% responsibility and immediately moves to the solution.
cloze_setup: An apology is about [______], not [______].
scenario_text: Team: 'The server went down because of that code you pushed.'

options.text: That was my mistake. I own that. Here is how I'm fixing it and how we'll prevent it next time.
options.feedback: Perfect. This actually increases your status. People trust leaders who admit mistakes.

options.text: Well, the documentation was confusing and nobody told me the rules...
options.feedback: Total Failure. You sound like a child making excuses. You just lost all your 'Power Player' points.

x_ray: In the US, 'Mistakes' are okay. 'Excuses' are not. Own the error to end the conflict immediately.

title: The 'Hard' Feedback
image_description: 3D clay style, you are a Lead now. You are talking to a junior developer who is making the same mistake every day. It's time to be firm.
strategic_brief: The 'Behavior' Lock. Focus on the 'Impact' of their actions on the team.
cloze_setup: Feedback should be [______] and [______]-focused.
scenario_text: How do you tell them they need to improve?

options.text: You're doing great, but maybe try to check your code more?
options.feedback: Too soft. They won't take you seriously. This is a 'Sandwich' with no meat.

options.text: I've noticed a pattern in your PRs. This is slowing the team down. What can we do to fix this by next week?
options.feedback: Strong Leadership. You stated the fact, the impact, and a deadline for a solution.

x_ray: Good leaders protect the team's standards. High-vibe conflict means being honest enough to help someone grow.

title: Escalating a Problem
image_description: 3D clay style, sitting at a desk, typing a message to your manager. A coworker is being consistently rude and it's affecting your work.
strategic_brief: The Business Case for HR. Don't complain about 'Feelings.' Complain about 'Productivity.'
cloze_setup: Escalation is a [______] tool, not a [______] one.
scenario_text: How do you tell your boss about the rude coworker?

options.text: He's mean and I don't like working with him. He makes me sad.
options.feedback: This sounds like school drama. It won't get you a professional result.

options.text: I'm concerned about the communication friction with [Name]. It's starting to delay our sprints. Can we discuss a solution?
options.feedback: Perfect. You linked the behavior to 'Delaying Sprints.' Now it's a business problem they must solve.

x_ray: Managers care about 'Results.' If you frame conflict as an obstacle to results, they will move mountains for you.

title: Negotiating a Deadline
image_description: 3D clay style, a calendar on a screen with 'Launch Day' highlighted in red. It is clearly impossible to meet the date.
strategic_brief: Under-promise, Over-deliver. It is better to have a 'Hard Fight' now than a 'Hard Failure' later.
cloze_setup: A [______] deadline is better than a [______] promise.
scenario_text: The client wants it by Friday. You know it takes until Tuesday. What do you do?

options.text: Say 'Okay' and then apologize on Friday when it's not done.
options.feedback: This is how you get fired. You just lost all your professional credibility.

options.text: If we want this done right, we need until Tuesday. I'm not comfortable rushing the testing phase.
options.feedback: Strong. You stood up for 'Quality.' This is what a Senior professional does.

x_ray: American clients value 'Predictability' over 'Politeness.' Tell them the truth today so they can plan for it.

title: The Heated Brainstorm
image_description: 3D clay style, 5 people in a room with a whiteboard. Ideas are flying and people are disagreeing loudly. It feels intense.
strategic_brief: Passionate, Not Angry. In the US, a loud debate is often a sign of a 'Healthy' team. Don't hide from the noise.
cloze_setup: High intensity is a sign of [______] to the [______].
scenario_text: Everyone is arguing. You have a third option that solves both sides. How do you lead the room?

options.text: Stand up, go to the whiteboard, and say: 'I think I see a middle path. Can I show you guys something?'
options.feedback: MASTERFUL. You just took control of the room. You are officially a Power Player.

options.text: Sit in the corner and wait for them to stop yelling.
options.feedback: You're invisible. You missed the chance to be the 'Hero' who solved the conflict.

x_ray: Conflict is the 'Fire' that makes the 'Steel' of a great project. Jump into the fire to lead the way out.

title: The Elevator Pitch
image_description: 3D clay style, inside a sleek elevator with glass doors. You are standing next to a Senior VP who just asked 'So, what are you working on?'
strategic_brief: The 30-Second ROI. An executive doesn't want a status report; they want to know the 'Impact' you are creating for the business.
cloze_setup: My pitch should focus on the [______] I provide, not the [______] I perform.
scenario_text: The VP smiles: 'I've heard your name in a few meetings. What's your focus lately?'

options.text: I'm mostly just fixing bugs in the CSS and updating some Figma files.
options.feedback: Too small. You sound like a 'Worker,' not a 'Partner.' Use higher-level language.

options.text: I’m leading the UI overhaul to improve our conversion rates. We’re already seeing a 10% lift in the pilot.
options.feedback: Perfect! You linked your work to a business metric (Conversion) and showed leadership.

x_ray: Executives think in 'Numbers' and 'Growth.' If you talk their language, they remember your name.

title: The Strategic 'Humble-Brag'
image_description: 3D clay style, a LinkedIn post on a smartphone screen. It has a photo of you receiving a small internal award. You are writing the caption.
strategic_brief: Visibility is Credibility. Sharing wins in the US isn't 'Arrogance'—it's 'Career Documentation.'
cloze_setup: I should share [______] to build [______] with people outside my immediate team.
scenario_text: You just won 'Employee of the Month.' How do you share it on the company Slack?

options.text: Say nothing. If I'm good, people will eventually find out.
options.feedback: Wrong. Silence in the US is often invisible. Nobody has time to hunt for your achievements.

options.text: So honored to win this! Huge thanks to the team for supporting the new design system launch. We're just getting started!
options.feedback: Excellent. You shared the win but redirected the praise to the 'Team' and the 'Project.'

x_ray: The 'Humble-Brag' formula: [Achievement] + [Gratitude for Team] + [Future Goal].

title: The Event Exit Strategy
image_description: 3D clay style, a networking mixer with people holding wine glasses. You are talking to someone, but the conversation is dead and you want to meet someone else.
strategic_brief: Protecting Your Time. Power Players know how to end a conversation without being rude so they can keep networking.
cloze_setup: Ending a [______] is a sign of [______] for both people's time.
scenario_text: You've been talking to a vendor for 10 minutes. How do you leave?

options.text: It was great meeting you! I’m going to go make a few more rounds, but let's connect on LinkedIn.
options.feedback: Polite and professional. You gave a clear reason and a 'Next Step' (LinkedIn).

options.text: I have to go to the bathroom now. Bye.
options.feedback: Childish. Everyone knows you're just running away. Use the 'Making Rounds' excuse instead.

x_ray: In the US, 'Making the rounds' is a standard networking phrase that implies you are a busy, in-demand professional.

title: The Internal Interview Vibe
image_description: 3D clay style, a formal office setting. You are sitting across from a manager from a *different* department. You want to transfer to their team.
strategic_brief: Cultural Translation. You aren't just a 'worker'; you are a 'Solution.' Match your skills to their specific pain points.
cloze_setup: I must translate my [______] into their [______].
scenario_text: The Manager asks: 'Why should I bring you onto the Product team?'

options.text: I'm a very hard worker and I always do what I'm told.
options.feedback: Too passive. They want a 'Leader' and a 'Thinker,' not just a 'Doer.'

options.text: I've seen how the dev team struggles with design handoffs. I want to use my UI/UX background to bridge that gap and speed up our shipping.
options.feedback: Perfect. You identified a 'Problem' (Handoffs) and offered yourself as the 'Bridge.'

x_ray: Internal mobility in the US is about 'Internal Marketing.' Show them how you make their life easier.

title: The 'Stay Top of Mind' Follow-up
image_description: 3D clay style, a laptop screen with a drafted email to a mentor you haven't talked to in 3 months.
strategic_brief: Low-Pressure Nurturing. Networking is a long game. A 2-sentence 'Hello' keeps the door open.
cloze_setup: Follow-ups should be [______] and [______]-free.
scenario_text: What do you write to a mentor just to say hello?

options.text: Hey! Just wanted to share that I used your advice on [Project] and it worked! Hope you're doing well.
options.feedback: Perfect. You gave them a 'Win' based on their advice. Mentors love feeling helpful.

options.text: Hi, do you have any job openings for me yet? I am still waiting.
options.feedback: Desperate and transactional. You just burned a bridge. Never ask for a favor in every email.

x_ray: The best time to network is when you *don't* need a job. It builds a foundation of genuine trust.

title: Public Speaking 'Vibe'
image_description: 3D clay style, standing behind a podium in a small hall. People are looking at you. You are about to present.
strategic_brief: The Enthusiasm Gap. In the US, if you aren't 'Excited' about your topic, the audience will be 'Bored.'
cloze_setup: My [______] is more important than my [______] during a talk.
scenario_text: How do you start your presentation?

options.text: Hi. My name is Han. Today I will talk about user research. (Monotone voice).
options.feedback: Too dry. You've already lost the room. Use a 'Hook.'

options.text: I’m so excited to share what we found! We talked to 50 users, and what they said surprised us...
options.feedback: Great! 'Excited' and 'Surprised' are high-vibe words that grab attention.

x_ray: American audiences crave 'Engagement.' Even if you are nervous, fake the energy until you feel it.

title: The Power Introduction
image_description: 3D clay style, you are introducing two people at a conference. One is a high-level CEO, the other is your talented friend.
strategic_brief: The 'Context' Gift. Don't just give names; give them a reason to talk to each other.
cloze_setup: A good intro provides a [______] between two [______].
scenario_text: How do you introduce your friend to the CEO?

options.text: CEO, this is my friend Min. Min, this is the CEO.
options.feedback: Lazy. Now they have to do the hard work of finding a topic. Help them!

options.text: Sarah, I'd like you to meet Min. He's the one who built that AI tool I was telling you about.
options.feedback: Masterful. You gave them a 'Conversation Hook' immediately. You are a 'Connector.'

x_ray: Being a 'Connector' is a high-status role. When you help others network, you increase your own value.

title: The Thought Leader Comment
image_description: 3D clay style, a tablet screen showing a post by a famous industry leader. You want to leave a comment that gets noticed.
strategic_brief: Insight over Flattery. Don't just say 'Great post.' Add a perspective that shows you are a peer.
cloze_setup: I should add [______] to the [______] rather than just agreeing.
scenario_text: An industry leader posts about the future of UI. What is your comment?

options.text: Great post! Thanks for sharing!
options.feedback: Generic. Nobody will click your profile after reading this.

options.text: Really interesting point on [X]. I've seen that in my own work at [Company], specifically when we tried [Y].
options.feedback: Excellent. You showed you are 'In the trenches' doing real work.

x_ray: Social media for professionals is about 'Proof of Work.' Every comment is a tiny resume.

title: Asking for Mentorship
image_description: 3D clay style, sitting in a coffee shop with a senior professional you admire. You want them to be your official mentor.
strategic_brief: The Specific Ask. Don't ask 'Will you be my mentor?' (Too heavy). Ask for 'Time' on a 'Topic.'
cloze_setup: Mentorship should start with a [______] request for [______].
scenario_text: How do you ask for their help?

options.text: Can you please teach me everything you know about being a manager?
options.feedback: Too much pressure. They'll say no because it sounds like a part-time job.

options.text: I really admire how you handle stakeholders. Could I buy you a coffee once a month to get your take on my current projects?
options.feedback: Perfect. You identified their 'Superpower' and asked for a small, defined amount of time.

x_ray: In the US, people love to give advice but they hate to give 'Commitment.' Start small and build the habit.

title: The High-Stakes Interview
image_description: 3D clay style, 3 people sitting across a table from you. It's the final round for your dream job. They ask: 'Why should we hire you over the other candidates?'
strategic_brief: The Culture-Fit Closer. At this level, everyone has the skills. They are looking for the 'Vibe' and 'Mission Alignment.'
cloze_setup: I should focus on my [______] and my [______] with the team.
scenario_text: What is your closing argument?

options.text: Because I have the most years of experience and I am very loyal.
options.feedback: A bit boring. Loyalty is a 'given.' They want a 'Growth Engine.'

options.text: I bring a global perspective to UI that matches your expansion goals. I’m not just here to design; I’m here to help you scale.
options.feedback: BOOM. You just stopped being an 'Applicant' and started being a 'Strategic Asset.'

x_ray: In the final round, it’s not about 'Can you do the job?' it’s 'Do we want to win with you?'

title: Negotiating the Raise
image_description: 3D clay style, a private office with a view of a city skyline. You are sitting across from your boss. A document is on the table between you.
strategic_brief: The Market Value Logic. In the US, a raise isn't a reward for 'Time Served'; it's a 'Market Correction' for the value you provide.
cloze_setup: I should base my [______] on [______] data, not personal needs.
scenario_text: Boss: 'You've done great this year, Han. What are your expectations for compensation moving forward?'

options.text: I've been here a year and my rent went up, so I was hoping for a 10% raise.
options.feedback: Weak. Your rent is not the company's problem. You sound like you're asking for a favor, not a promotion.

options.text: Based on the 20% increase in team velocity I led this year, I'm looking for a salary that reflects that impact.
options.feedback: Masterful. You linked the money to the 'Impact' (Velocity). This is how Power Players negotiate.

x_ray: Americans respect people who know their 'Market Value.' If you don't ask for it, they assume you don't know it.

title: The 'Classy' Exit
image_description: 3D clay style, you are standing in your manager's office. You just accepted a better job offer elsewhere. It's time to give your notice.
strategic_brief: Protecting the Bridge. The tech world is small. How you leave a job is how you are remembered for the next 10 years.
cloze_setup: I should focus on [______] and a smooth [______].
scenario_text: Boss: 'I'm sad to see you go. How do you want to handle your last two weeks?'

options.text: I'm checking out now. I'll just do the bare minimum until Friday.
options.feedback: Bridge Burner. You just erased 2 years of good work in 2 seconds. Word will travel.

options.text: I want to make sure the team is set up for success. I’ve already started a transition doc for my replacement.
options.feedback: Elite. You are leaving as a 'Hero.' This boss will hire you again in 5 years at a higher level.

x_ray: The 'Two-Week Notice' is a test of character. Leaving with a 'Succession Plan' is the ultimate professional move.

title: Managing Up
image_description: 3D clay style, you are in a 1-on-1. Your boss is overwhelmed and forgetting things. You need to lead your leader.
strategic_brief: The Executive Assistant Mindset. Don't wait for orders. Anticipate their needs and provide solutions before they ask.
cloze_setup: Managing up is about reducing my boss's [______] [______].
scenario_text: Boss: 'Uh, I forgot... did we decide on the budget for the new tool?'

options.text: Yes, we talked about it Tuesday. Don't you remember?
options.feedback: Too aggressive. You made them feel stupid. Not a smart move for your career.

options.text: I have the notes right here! I’ve outlined the two options we liked. Want me to send over the summary?
options.feedback: Perfect. You became their 'Memory.' You are now indispensable.

x_ray: A 'Power Player' makes their boss look good. When your boss succeeds because of you, you rise with them.

title: Strategic Silence
image_description: 3D clay style, a high-level boardroom. Everyone is arguing about a mistake. You know the answer, but the room is too loud.
strategic_brief: The Power of the Pause. Sometimes, the most influential person in the room is the one who says the least.
cloze_setup: I should wait for the [______] to peak before I provide the [______].
scenario_text: The argument is going in circles. When do you speak?

options.text: Wait until there is a tired silence, then speak calmly and slowly.
options.feedback: Masterful. Silence acts as a spotlight. When you finally speak, everyone will lean in.

options.text: Try to shout over everyone so they know you have the answer.
options.feedback: You just added to the noise. You look like a participant, not a leader.

x_ray: In the US, 'Noise' is cheap. 'Clarity' is expensive. Be the one who provides clarity.

title: American Storytelling
image_description: 3D clay style, you are giving a presentation to the whole company. There are no bullet points on the slide, just a powerful photo.
strategic_brief: Data vs. Narrative. Americans don't follow 'Charts'; they follow 'Stories.' Turn your project into a journey.
cloze_setup: I should lead with the [______] problem, then show the [______] solution.
scenario_text: How do you start your talk about the new app update?

options.text: First, I'll show you the system architecture and the database schema.
options.feedback: Boring. You're talking to their brains, not their hearts. They will check their phones in 5 minutes.

options.text: I want to tell you about a user named 'Minsu.' He spent 3 hours trying to do [Task]. Here’s how we fixed that.
options.feedback: Brilliant. You made it personal. Now they care about the technical details because they care about Minsu.

x_ray: The 'Hero's Journey' is the default American logic. Your user is the hero; your app is the sword.

title: Regional Vibe Shifting
image_description: 3D clay style, you are traveling to a branch office in New York (or California). The vibe is different from your home office in Texas.
strategic_brief: The Cultural Chameleon. US 'Vibe' changes by region. NYC is fast/blunt; SF is casual/visionary; The South is polite/indirect.
